title-group.article-title: Risk prediction models for blood transfusion in patients undergoing total hip and knee arthroplasty: a systematic review and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: aEvidence-based Medicine Center, Luoyang Orthopedic Hospital of Henan Province. Orthopedic Hospital of Henan Province, Luoyang, Henan Province, People’s Republic of China
bDepartment of Nursing, Fujian University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Fuzhou, Fujian Province, People’s Republic of China
cDepartment of Nursing, Henan University of Chinese Medicine, Zhengzhou, Henan Province, People’s Republic of China

fn.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/07853890.2025.2610097.
These authors contributed equally to this study.

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Xiaolong Wu LYZG.YangYX@outlook.com No. 82, Qiming South Road, Luoyang City, Henan Province471002, People’s Republic of China

event.event-desc: published online in a building issue

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: To systematically review and evaluate published risk prediction models for perioperative blood transfusion in patients undergoing total hip or knee arthroplasty (THA/TKA).

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We systematically searched PubMed, Web of Science, the Cochrane Library, and Embase from inception to May 31, 2025. Two researchers independently screened the literature, extracted data, and assessed the risk of bias and applicability using the Prediction model Risk Of Bias Assessment Tool (PROBAST). The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) values were pooled via a meta-analysis using Stata 18.0.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: d Fourteen studies containing 36 prediction models were included. The incidence of blood transfusion among THA/TKA patients ranged from 3.2% to 30.8%. Preoperative hemoglobin (Hb) level, tranexamic acid (TXA) use, operative duration, intraoperative blood loss, and age were the most frequently incorporated predictors. Model sensitivity ranged from 58% to 94.5%, and specificity ranged from 71.3% to 94%. Meta-analysis showed that the pooled AUC value of the 13 validated models was 0.87 (95% CI: 0.85-0.90), suggesting good discriminatory performance. All models were rated as having a high risk of bias. The applicability of four studies was rated as unclear.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Although the included studies demonstrated promising discriminative ability of prediction models for blood transfusion in THA/TKA, all were assessed as having a high risk of bias using the PROBAST tool. Therefore, future research should prioritize the development of models with larger sample sizes, rigorous study designs, and multicenter external validation.

institution-wrap.institution: the Research Project on Traditional Chinese Medicine Culture and Management in Henan Province
the Henan Provincial Special Research Project for Traditional Chinese Medicine

funding-group.funding-statement: This work was supported by the Research Project on Traditional Chinese Medicine Culture and Management in Henan Province [grant numbers TCM2025039]; and the Henan Provincial Special Research Project for Traditional Chinese Medicine [grant numbers 2025ZY3077].

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Total hip arthroplasty (THA) and total knee arthroplasty (TKA) are definitive surgical interventions for end-stage osteoarthritis of the hip and knee joints [1,2]. These procedures restore joint function by replacing damaged articular surfaces with prosthetic implants [3]. Driven by an aging population, the global volume of THA and TKA surgeries continues to rise [4,5]. However, substantial intraoperative blood loss is a nearly unavoidable consequence of these procedures [6]. Literature reports indicate that total perioperative blood loss for THA and TKA typically ranges between 700 and 2000 mL [7–9]. This blood loss not only hinders postoperative joint functional recovery but also increases the likelihood of requiring transfusion. Although allogeneic blood transfusion can partially address anemia, it carries significant risks, including allergic reactions, transfusion-associated adverse events, and disease transmission, while also imposing substantial economic burdens on healthcare systems [10–12]. Consequently, the early identification of patients at high risk for transfusion following THA/TKA is crucial for optimizing clinical management, reducing complication rates, and enhancing postoperative recovery.

Risk prediction models, which integrate multiple clinical and non-clinical factors to estimate the probability of specific future outcomes, are widely employed in medical practice [13,14]. In recent years, the number of transfusion risk prediction models for patients undergoing THA/TKA has increased significantly. These tools facilitate early identification of high-risk patients, enable intensified perioperative monitoring, and thereby prevent transfusion-related adverse events [15]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no systematic evaluation has yet focused specifically on transfusion risk prediction models within the THA/TKA patient population. To this end, this study conducts a systematic review, utilizing the PROBAST tool to comprehensively assess the fundamental characteristics, predictive performance, and methodological quality of existing models, aiming to provide an evidence-based foundation for selecting or developing more effective prediction tools and achieving early and accurate identification of transfusion risk in THA/TKA patients.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) 2020 guidelines, and its protocol was prospectively registered with PROSPERO (CRD420251064162) on 30 May 2025.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: Two investigators independently conducted a systematic search of the databases PubMed, Web of Science, the Cochrane Library, and Embase from their inception to May 31, 2025. The search strategy utilized a combination of MeSH terms and keywords, including but not limited to: ‘Arthroplasty, Replacement’, ‘Arthroplasty, Replacement, Hip’, ‘Arthroplasty, Replacement, Knee’, ‘Blood Transfusion’, ‘Risk Factors’, and ‘Risk Prediction Model’. The complete search strategies for each database are detailed in Appendix 1. Additionally, the reference lists of all included studies and relevant reviews were manually screened to identify any potentially eligible articles.

This systematic review adopted the PICOTS framework as recommended by the Critical Appraisal and Data Extraction for Systematic Reviews of Prediction Modeling Studies (CHARMS) checklist [16]. The key components of this framework are defined as follows:
P (Population): Patients undergoing THA or TKA.
I (Intervention): Risk prediction models developed to estimate the risk of requiring perioperative blood transfusion in patients undergoing THA or TKA.
C (Comparator): Not applicable.
O (Outcome): Perioperative blood transfusion.
T (Timing): During hospitalization.
S (Setting): Hospital setting.

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: The inclusion criteria for studies were as follows: (1) Patients undergoing THA/TKA; (2) Development and/or validation of risk prediction models for perioperative transfusion risk; (3) Case-control, cross-sectional, and cohort study design; (4) English-language publications.

The exclusion criteria were: (1) Studies solely analyzing risk factors without model development; (2) Transfusion as a secondary outcome among multiple complications; (3) Models comprising ≤2 predictors; (4) Unavailability of full text or incomplete data.

sec.title: Study selection and data extraction
sec.p: Two investigators independently performed study selection and data extraction. After deduplication using EndNote 21, titles, abstracts, and full texts were sequentially screened against the eligibility criteria. To minimize omissions, citation tracking and hand-searched reference lists of included articles were conducted. Any disagreements were resolved by consensus or through consultation with a third investigator.

sec.title: Risk of bias and application assessment
sec.p: Two reviewers independently assessed the risk of bias and applicability of included studies using the PROBAST tool [17], with discrepancies resolved through consultation with a third reviewer. This tool evaluates risk of bias across four domains (participants, predictors, outcome, and analysis), while applicability was assessed only for the first three [18]. Each domain comprises signaling questions with response options of ‘yes,’ ‘probably yes,’ ‘probably no,’ ‘no,’ and ‘no information.’ A domain was classified as high risk if any question was answered ‘no’ or ‘probably no’; as low risk if all questions were answered ‘yes’ or ‘probably yes’; and as unclear if any question was answered ‘no information.’ The overall risk of bias for a study was considered low only when all four domains were rated as low risk.

sec.title: Data analyses
sec.p: All statistical analyses were conducted using Stata 18.0. Given the substantial heterogeneity among the included studies in design, model types, and predictors, we pooled the AUC values using the DerSimonian–Laird random-effects model and applied the Hartung–Knapp–Sidik–Jonkman (HKSJ) method to calculate the 95% confidence intervals, thereby obtaining more robust inferences under the limited number of studies [19]. For studies that did not directly report AUC CIs or standard errors, estimates were derived using the Hanley–McNeil method [20,21]. Heterogeneity was quantified with the I2 statistic and Cochran’s Q test, using cutoffs of 25%, 50%, and 75% for low, moderate, and high heterogeneity, respectively. When substantial heterogeneity was present (I2 > 50%), sensitivity analyses were conducted to explore its sources. Publication bias was evaluated using Egger’s regression test, with a P value >0.05 indicating no significant bias.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: Figure 1 presents the PRISMA [22] flowchart of the study selection process. A total of 2,164 records were identified through database searching, from which 573 duplicates were removed. After reviewing titles and abstracts, 1,478 publications were excluded. Following full-text assessment, 99 articles were excluded, resulting in the final inclusion of 14 articles for review [10,23–35].

caption.p: PRISMA flowchart of literature search and selection.

sec.title: Basic characteristics of included studies
sec.p: Table 1 summarizes the design and participant characteristics of the 14 included studies. Published from 2018 to 2025, nine studies were conducted in China [10,23,25–27,31,33–35], three in the United States [28–30], one in South Korea [32], and one in France [24]. Thirteen were retrospective cohort studies [10,23–34] and one a prospective cohort study [35]. Regarding the study population, five studies focused solely on THA patients [23,26,27,30,34], six exclusively on TKA patients [24,25,28,29,32,33], and three included both THA and TKA patients [10,31,35]. Sample sizes ranged from 224 to 15,187 participants, and the reported blood transfusion rates ranged between 3.2% and 30.8%.

tr.td: the Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University
the Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University
The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University
the First People’s Hospital of Nanning
the Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University

fn.p: Note. THA, total hip arthroplasty; TKA, total knee arthroplasty; TF, transfusion; NTF, non-transfusion.

sec.title: Development and validation of risk predictive models
sec.p: Table 2 summarizes key parameters pertaining to model development and validation across the 14 included studies, which collectively yielded 36 risk prediction models for transfusion in THA/TKA patients [10,23–35]. The number of candidate predictors per study ranged from 8 to 44, with variable selection performed via logistic regression (LR), LASSO, or recursive feature elimination. Continuous variables were predominantly retained in their native form, with only one study dichotomizing them [23]. Missing data were predominantly excluded from analysis. Development sample sizes ranged from 224 to 15,187 cases, whereas validation cohorts comprised 100 to 15,187 cases. Regarding model construction, LR served as the primary technique, followed by random forest and support vector machine algorithms. All models underwent validation, with four studies conducting external validation [26,32–34] and ten studies internal validation [10,23–25,27–31,35].

caption.p: Development and validation of risk predictive models.

fn.p: Note. ‘–’, not reported; LR, logistic regression; LASSO, least absolute shrinkage and selection operator; RFE, recursive feature elimination; K-NN, K-nearest neighbors; RF, Random Forest; GBM, gradient boosting machine; LSTM, long short-term memory network; NB, naïve Bayes; SVM, support vector machine; DT, decision tree; MPNN, multilayer perceptron neural network; ANN, artificial neural network; SGB, stochastic gradient boosting; ENP, elastic-net penalized logistic regression; MLP, multilayer perceptron; GB, gradient boosting; DCA, decision curve analysis.

sec.title: Performance and predictors of risk prediction models
sec.p: Table 3 delineates key information regarding model performance and predictors. Development AUC values, reported in six studies, ranged from 0.80 to 0.978 [25,26,30,32–34], whereas validation AUCs were documented across all 14 studies, ranging from 0.66 to 0.97. Five studies further reported model sensitivity (58.0–94.5%) and specificity (71.3–94.0%) [10,24,28,31,32]. Calibration was assessed in seven studies through calibration curves, decision curve analysis, or Brier score [23,25–27,30,33,34] The most prevalent predictors encompassed preoperative hemoglobin level, tranexamic acid administration, operative time, intraoperative blood loss, and patient age. With respect to model presentation formats, six studies employed nomograms [23,25–27,33,34], four developed web-based calculators [10,24,26,32],and one utilized a risk score formula [28].

caption.p: Performance and predictors of risk prediction models.

tr.td: aFemale; ASA II; ASA III; ASA IV; Operative time; Drain use; IBLbHigher preoperative Hb; TXA use
aLonger operation time; Simultaneous bilateral THA; Greater blood loss; comorbid CHD; Lower preoperative Hb; Lower preoperative PLT; Lower weightbTXA use; Nongeneral anesthesia; Smoking
Simultaneous bilateral TKA; CHD; Hb level; Age; BMI
Preoperative Hb; PLT; Surgery type; TXA; Age; Body weight
aAge 65–85; Female; ASA grade II or greater; Oprolonged operative time; Drain use; IBLbHigher preoperative Hb; Hct; BMI(≥24 kg/m2); TXA use

Preoperative Hb level; Preoperative creatinine level; Length of surgery; Simultaneous bilateral Surgery; TXA use; ASA score; Preoperative ALB level; Ethanol usage; Preoperative anticoagulation medications; Age; TKA type
Age; Sex; BMI; Preoperative Hb level; TXA use; ASA class; Operative time; Drain use
aFemale; Diabetes; General anesthesia; Bleeding disorders; No TXA usagebNormal preoperative PLT; Elevated Preoperative Hct(36%)
Timing of using a tourniquet, Drain use; TXA use; Preoperative ESR; HCT; Plasma ALB level
BMI; Operation duration; IBL; Anticoagulation history; TXA use; Preoperative Hb level; Preoperative ALB
Age; BMI; Hb; PLT; Bleeding volume; Urine volume
Concomitant use of clopidogrel; Preoperative Hb level; ASA class; Total estimated blood loss volume; TXA use
Age; BMI; TXA use; Preoperative Hb level; PLT
IBL; Hypertension; Postoperative drainage volume; Surgical type; Surgery duration; Pre­operative Hb

fn.p: Note. ‘a’, risk factors; ‘b’, protective factors; BMI, body mass index; TXA, tranexamic acid; ASA, American Society of Anesthesiologists; Hb, hemoglobin; PLT, platelet count; Hct, hematocrit; IBL, intraoperative blood loss; ESR, erythrocyte sedimentation rate; CHD, coronary heart disease; ALB, albumin; AUC, area under the curve. We considered AUC = 0.5–0.7 as poor discrimination, 0.7–0.8 as moderate discrimination, 0.8–0.9 as good discrimination, and 0.9–1.0 as excellent discrimination.

sec.title: Risk of bias and applicability
sec.p: All 14 studies evaluated using the PROBAST tool demonstrated a high risk of bias (ROB), indicating methodological shortcomings in development or validation that raise concerns about their real-world applicability. In the participant domain, 13 studies were rated as high ROB primarily due to retrospective cohort designs [10,23–34]. In the predictor domain, three studies utilizing multicenter data were assessed as high ROB [26,31,35], while the remaining 11 studies had unclear ROB because they failed to report whether predictors were assessed blinded to outcome data [10,23–25,27–30,32–34]. In the outcome domain, 13 studies had unclear ROB due to unreported timeframes between predictor assessment and outcome determination [10,23–26,28–35]. In the analysis domain, four studies had insufficient sample sizes (<20 events per variable) [23,25–27]; one study relied exclusively on literature review and expert opinion for variable selection [28]; seven studies failed to report calibration methods [10,24,28,29,31,32,35]; six studies excluded patients with missing data [23,25,26,31,33,34]; one study inappropriately categorized continuous variables into discrete groups [23]; and three studies conducted both model development and validation on the same dataset [23,27,35]. Regarding applicability, ten studies demonstrated low ROB, while four had unclear overall applicability due to unreported outcome definitions [10,25,27,30]. The results of the risk of bias and applicability evaluation using the PROBAST are summarized in Figures 2 and 3.

caption.p: PROBAST results of the included studies. Note. L, low risk of bias; H, high risk of bias; N, unclear risk of bias.

sec.title: Meta-analysis of validated models
sec.p: In the meta-analysis of validation AUC, if a study employed multiple modeling approaches within the same cohort, only the best-performing model meeting the inclusion criteria was selected. All 14 identified studies reported validation AUC; however, one was excluded due to insufficient data for calculating CIs [26], resulting in a final inclusion of 13 studies [10,23–25,27–35]. The random-effects model produced a pooled AUC of 0.87 (95% CI: 0.85–0.90), demonstrating robust overall discriminative ability. Nevertheless, significant heterogeneity was observed across the studies (I2 = 80.3%, p < 0.001; Supplementary Figure 1).

Subgroup analysis indicated that heterogeneity was primarily driven by studies employing internal validation (I2 = 84.9%) and those utilizing machine learning (ML) models (I2 = 88.7%). Further pooled analysis revealed that the subgroup of ‘ML methods with internal validation’ exhibited the most pronounced heterogeneity (I2 = 90.5%). Despite marked differences in heterogeneity across subgroups, the pooled AUCs showed no statistically significant differences. Furthermore, subgroup analysis based on publication year did not reveal significant between-group differences. Subgroup forest plots are shown in Supplementary Figures 2–8.

caption.p: Results of Bias and applicability assessment of 14 studies. Note. (A) Overall rate of ROB based on PROBAST. (B) Overall rate of applicability based on PROBAST.

sec.p: Sensitivity analysis confirmed the stability of the pooled estimate upon sequential exclusion of each study (Supplementary Figure 9). Additionally, Egger’s test indicated no substantial publication bias (p = 0.716; Supplementary Figure 10).

sec.title: The included prediction models demonstrated good predictive performance but high overall risk of bias
sec.p: This systematic review included 14 studies encompassing 36 prediction models. The meta-analysis yielded a pooled AUC of 0.87 (95% CI: 0.85–0.90), indicating good overall discriminative ability. However, the PROBAST assessment revealed a high overall risk of bias, primarily originating from the Participants and Analysis domains, which substantially limits their clinical utility. Therefore, future research should prioritize the following: employing multicenter prospective cohorts to mitigate selection bias; maintaining an events per variable (EPV) of ≥20 to prevent overfitting; and implementing blinding during outcome and predictor assessment. Furthermore, when handling missing data, comprehensive reporting of the reasons for and extent of missingness is essential to enhance model robustness and transparency. Notably, only four studies conducted external validation. Prior to widespread clinical implementation, rigorous external validation in independent populations is essential to ensure the reproducibility, reliability, and generalizability of models for real-world decision-making. In conclusion, future research should adhere to the PROBAST methodological standards and the Transparent Reporting of a Multivariable Prediction Model for Individual Prognosis or Diagnosis (TRIPOD) reporting guidelines to systematically improve study design and methodological quality for developing reliable clinical prediction models.

sec.title: Analysis of predictors in blood transfusion risk prediction models for THA/TKA patients
sec.p: The predictors included in each model are not entirely the same. Preoperative Hb levels, TXA use, operative duration, intraoperative blood loss, and age emerged as the most frequently incorporated predictors. Among the 14 studies, preoperative Hb level was identified as a significant predictor in 12 studies [10,23,24,26–29,31–35]. Inadequate preoperative Hb reserves increase susceptibility to erythrocyte loss during surgery, frequently necessitating transfusion to maintain physiological function. Consistent evidence indicates that lower preoperative Hb levels are associated with higher transfusion rates [36–39]. TXA administration was included as a predictor in 11 studies [23–26,28–32,34,35]. As an antifibrinolytic agent, TXA blocks the lysine-binding sites on plasminogen, inhibiting plasmin formation [40], and has demonstrated efficacy in reducing blood loss and transfusion rates in orthopedic surgery [41–43]. Furthermore, surgical duration [10,26,28,29,31,34,35], intraoperative blood loss [10,23,26,27,30,31,34], and age [24,27–29,31–33] were each significant predictors in seven studies.

The identification of reliable predictors is essential for developing robust transfusion risk models, which provide accurate probability estimates to guide targeted interventions. Integrating these models into preoperative management and transfusion decision-making significantly enhances their clinical utility. For example, when embedded within electronic health record systems, they can automatically generate risk scores and trigger early warnings, thereby directing precise clinical actions such as preoperative anemia management, prophylactic medication, and blood preparation. This integrated strategy not only improves patient outcomes through timely intervention but also optimizes blood resource allocation, yielding substantial clinical and economic benefits. Future research should focus on refining risk-stratified models through in-depth analysis of these core predictors.

sec.title: Trends and advancements in transfusion risk prediction models for THA/TKA patients
sec.p: The literature included in this study has focused on the past 7 years, indicating a heightened clinical focus on transfusion risk prediction models for THA and TKA patients. Driven by the increasing application of ML in medicine, algorithms such as decision trees, support vector machines, random forests, and k-nearest neighbors are increasingly leveraged in predictive model development. Among the 14 included studies, six employed traditional LR [23,25,27,28,33,34], whereas eight applied other ML algorithms [10,24,26,29–32,35].

ML is often considered superior to traditional LR in handling real-world data, which frequently exhibit nonlinearity and complex feature interrelationships, thereby enabling ML to process large-scale, high-dimensional datasets efficiently [44]. However, ML models do not consistently outperform traditional methods [45]. In fact, in some cases, traditional LR models may be simpler and more efficient. This is primarily due to their straightforward form, which enhances interpretability and aids clinical understanding. Additionally, traditional LR achieves efficient convergence and yields stable results even with limited datasets [46], with the resultant regression coefficients directly quantifying the impact of each variable on the outcome. Conversely, ML models are typically characterized by greater complexity and diminished interpretability, often referred to as ‘black-box’ systems [47]. However, ongoing research is dedicated to enhancing the interpretability of these complex algorithms. Consequently, researchers must meticulously evaluate methodological trade-offs based on specific data characteristics. To optimize predictive performance and generalization capability, we recommend combining multiple modeling approaches during prediction model development.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This systematic review has several inherent limitations. First, restriction to English-language publications may introduce a language bias, potentially compromising the generalizability of the findings. Second, as most studies focused on mainland Chinese populations, the conclusions may have limited generalizability to other countries, necessitating population-specific recalibration. Finally, insufficient reporting of external validation and calibration for most models constrains their external generalizability. Future research should address these limitations through rigorous methodological refinements to enhance scientific validity and clinical applicability.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study systematically reviewed blood transfusion risk prediction models for patients undergoing TKA/THA. The review included 14 articles, which encompassed 36 distinct models. Meta-analysis of model validation AUCs revealed consistent good predictive performance across all models. Preoperative hemoglobin levels, TXA administration, surgical duration, intraoperative blood loss, and age emerged as the five most commonly utilized predictors. However, PROBAST assessment revealed a high risk of bias for all models, with four studies raising concerns regarding applicability, indicating that current models fall short of meeting PROBAST standards. To mitigate bias risks and enhance clinical utility, researchers should rigorously adhere to PROBAST methodological standards and TRIPOD guidelines during model development and reporting. Furthermore, multicenter clinical studies with larger sample sizes are warranted to provide robust evidence-based guidance for the early identification and prevention of blood transfusion in THA/TKA patients.

sec.title: Ethics statement
sec.p: Ethical approval for this study was considered unnecessary because it involved a review of peer-reviewed literature and did not directly involve patient-level data.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: The authors report there are no competing interests to declare.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article and its supplementary information files. Further data can be requested from the corresponding author.
